evidence: Multiple modules define large callback-bag runtimes with 10+ function fields plus custom defaulting/validation layers (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go, goseg/rectify/rectify.go, goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go, goseg/importer/importer.go).
The same runtime-shaping pattern is repeated manually: withDefaults/merge/validate and missing-callback string plumbing, indicating indirection growth without shared leverage.

Tests mirror this complexity by constructing many callback stubs just to reach behavior under test (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go, goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/docker_test.go).

Some abstractions are bypassed by concrete globals and concrete-only helper APIs, e.g. broadcast APIs mixing BroadcastStore with *broadcastStateRuntime-specific entry points (goseg/broadcast/transitions.go, goseg/broadcast/state.go).

Config patch behavior is split across multiple parallel declarations: section constants/descriptors in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, patch carrier structs and generic patch primitives in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`, and a separate field registry plus section mapping in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`.

Large manual field registries (`connectivityPatchFields`, `runtimePatchFields`, `penpaiPatchFields`, `authSessionPatchFields`) repeat the same parse/apply/merge shape, creating a broad shotgun-surgery surface for any config-key change.

Session state management duplicates collection/bookkeeping mechanics in two different modules (`goseg/session/connection_registry.go` and `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go`) with divergent semantics (dedupe in one path, raw append in another).

Transition runtime handlers in `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` and `goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go` mix transition wiring, persistence mutation, container orchestration, and event emission in the same functions.

Best-effort transition publishing is handled inconsistently: some paths propagate failure, while `schedulePack` (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`) and importer/rectify publish helpers only log and return success.

Boundary error context is inconsistent: `NetworkRuntime.KillContainerUsingPort` (`goseg/docker/network/network.go`) returns raw Docker client construction errors, while neighboring methods in the same file wrap with operation context.

Log-and-return patterns are repeated across startup/runtime loops (`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `goseg/routines/version.go`), causing duplicate reporting and uneven failure semantics.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency injection is implemented as repeated large callback bags, creating high indirection cost with low reuse.
evidence: startupInitRuntime and startBackgroundServicesRuntime carry long function-field lists and manual withDefaults/validate plumbing (startup_orchestrator.go:117-274, 351-539).
RectifyRuntime repeats the same pattern with mergeRectifyRuntime + validate over many callbacks (rectify.go:24-182).
workflowRuntime repeats defaults/merge/validate for many function seams (operations_runtime.go:23-171).
importerRuntime follows the same callback-bag + segmented validators pattern (importer.go:104-260).
main.go introduces another runtime bag for bootstrap/c2c/server wiring (main.go:65-148, 172-263).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared runtime-composition pattern per bounded context: group dependencies into smaller role interfaces (e.g., config, orchestration, transition-publish, filesystem), then compose a thin runtime from those interfaces. Replace repeated withDefaults/validate code with one generic dependency validator/helper used across modules.

findings.summary: Abstraction shape leaks into tests, requiring broad stub scaffolding to test narrow behaviors.
evidence: startup_orchestrator_test constructs large runtime structs with many callbacks for single test scenarios (startup_orchestrator_test.go:109-127, 193-207).
subsystem docker tests require full test runtime population via testDockerRoutineRuntime before behavior assertions (docker_test.go:21-55).
main tests repeatedly build nested runtime structs (c2cRuntime/connectivity/device/mode) to isolate one branch (main_test.go:125-209).
findings.suggestion: Define slimmer collaborator interfaces at call sites (per behavior path) and provide focused test doubles for those interfaces. Keep one integration-level runtime-bag test, but move most unit tests to small interface contracts.

findings.summary: Several runtime seams force artificial error-wrapping closures for operations that are naturally non-erroring, weakening interface honesty.
evidence: Rectify runtime wraps non-error snapshot getters into `(value, error)` closures that always return nil error (rectify.go:50-64).

Importer runtime wraps non-error operations in error-returning callbacks (e.g., updateContainerFn and wait-for-boot/remote wrappers) that always return nil except delegated branch (importer.go:154-171).
findings.suggestion: Split read-only/value seams from failure-prone seams. Use plain value-returning function types where failures are impossible, and keep error-returning signatures only on true I/O/boundary operations.

findings.summary: Broadcast abstractions expose both interface-based and concrete-runtime APIs, causing leaky boundaries.
evidence: BroadcastStore interface exists for transition operations (transitions.go:15-21), but state helpers still accept/return concrete `*broadcastStateRuntime` (state.go:118-131, 134-195).
Rectify paths still directly reach default singleton broadcast runtime in helper flows (rectify.go:252-257), bypassing injected runtime in parts of the package.
findings.suggestion: Standardize broadcast access behind BroadcastStore (or a small pair of interfaces for state + schedule bus), and remove concrete pointer-based helper entry points from package-level APIs. Ensure helper paths accept injected runtime consistently instead of default singleton access.

findings.summary: Config patching is defined in parallel structures across files, creating a high-risk shotgun-surgery design.
evidence: `SysConfigSection` and section descriptors are centralized in `structs/configs.go`, but patch-domain mapping is re-declared in `sysConfigSectionPatchSpecs` in `config_update_registry.go`.
`ConfPatch` embeds per-domain patch structs in `config_update_schema.go`, while field-level key metadata is independently maintained in `config_update_registry.go`.

Any new config section/field requires coordinated edits across section descriptors, patch structs, and registry entries, which indicates structural duplication rather than a single authoritative schema.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical schema (e.g., a section+field descriptor table) that generates/derives patch parsing, apply, and merge behavior; remove parallel hand-maintained section and field registries.

findings.summary: Field-level patch registry code repeats the same structural pattern extensively, obscuring intent and making drift likely.
evidence: Most entries in `connectivityPatchFields`, `runtimePatchFields`, `penpaiPatchFields`, and `authSessionPatchFields` repeat near-identical `key/patchField/target/parse/apply/merge` scaffolding.

Adapter helpers (`parseBoolField`, `parseIntField`, `parseStringField`, `patchFieldPointer`, `patchFieldValue`) mostly forward to primitives from `config_update_schema.go`, adding another indirection layer without reducing declaration volume.
Merge semantics are encoded as many micro-functions (`mergeStringSetIfNonEmpty`, `mergeIntSetIfNonZero`, `mergeBoolOr`, etc.), then re-selected manually per field, increasing policy scatter.
findings.suggestion: Move to a compact data-driven field spec format with reusable merge-policy enums/strategies so each field entry only specifies unique facts (key, section path, policy), not repeated function plumbing.

findings.summary: Session tracking logic is duplicated across registries with inconsistent collection semantics, weakening coherence of websocket lifecycle handling.
evidence: `connection_registry.go` implements dedupe and removal helpers (`appendMuConnIfMissing`, `removeConnFromBucket`, `cleanupStaleBucket`) for token-bucketed connection state.

`logstream_session_store.go` re-implements similar copy/remove bookkeeping for websocket sets/slices, but `AddSysLogSession` unconditionally appends, unlike dedupe-oriented behavior used in registry helpers.
Both files contain custom map/slice copy-and-filter loops for connection lifecycle state, but with different invariants and no shared abstraction for session collection operations.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared connection-collection component (dedupe, remove, copy, stale cleanup policies) and reuse it in both registry/store paths to enforce one lifecycle convention.

findings.summary: Ship transition handlers combine orchestration assembly, business validation, side effects, and persistence mutations in single functions.
evidence: `buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps` in `urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains state discovery, external calls (`click`/`docker`), config mutation, and event sequencing in one step builder.

`runStartramToggleWithRuntime` in `startram_lifecycle_runtime.go` constructs and executes a long heterogeneous workflow (config toggles, container operations, per-pier payload dispatch, rebuild/load operations) inline.

`runtime_ops.go` exposes very broad seam structs with many function fields, and transition handlers select and compose these imperatively per flow, reinforcing ad-hoc orchestration instead of a normalized command/step model.
findings.suggestion: Refactor transition flows into declarative command pipelines with isolated stages (validate, mutate-config, external-side-effects, emit-events), and centralize reusable step templates for shared operations.

findings.summary: Several state/transition publish paths swallow publish failures and report success, creating silent divergence between state changes and emitted events.
evidence: `schedulePack` persists schedule config, then if `broadcast.PublishSchedulePack("schedule")` fails it only logs and still returns `nil`.
`publishImportTransition` logs warning on publish failure and does not propagate an error to caller paths handling upload lifecycle state.
`publishUrbitServiceRegistrationTransitionWithRuntime` logs apply-transition failure and returns nothing, so callers cannot react or retry.
findings.suggestion: Define one explicit policy for event-publish failures (strict, best-effort-with-retry, or best-effort-with-observable status) and return a typed outcome/error from these helpers so callers can decide whether to fail, retry, or mark degraded state consistently.

findings.summary: Error context is inconsistently preserved at I/O/runtime boundaries, leading to mixed opaque and actionable failures.
evidence: `KillContainerUsingPort` returns raw `DockerClientNewFn` error (`return err`) while sibling methods in the same file wrap with operation-specific context.
`RemoveUrbitConfig` returns raw `os.Remove` error without path/pier context, unlike other config loaders/persistors that wrap with rich operation text.
`C2cCheckWith` logs activation failure and returns raw `err` from `isC2cMode`, while adjacent `setC2cMode` path wraps (`unable to set C2C mode: %w`).
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single boundary rule: always wrap external dependency errors with operation + entity context (and preserve `%w`), enforced via shared helper or lint rule for raw `return err` at package boundaries.

findings.summary: Multiple flows both log errors and propagate them, producing duplicate error surfaces and inconsistent ownership of error reporting.
evidence: `C2cCheckWith` logs `Error activating C2C mode` then returns the same error; upstream startup/main path also logs terminal errors.
`loadService` logs `failureMessage` and returns the same error, which higher-level orchestration can aggregate/log again.
`CheckVersionLoopWithContext` logs each updater failure, and outer subsystem starters also log when loop exits with error.
findings.suggestion: Enforce a single-owner logging contract (inner layers return wrapped errors; top-level boundary logs once). Remove intermediate error logs unless they add unique diagnostic context not available upstream.
